retrieved_docs.title: Death and Dying(Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The CDC collects and reports data about deaths in the United States of America annually. By assessing this information, trends can be identified and addressed. This information and its trends can also be used to develop new research, process improvement, and community programs. Number of deaths: 2,744,248 Death rate: 849.3 deaths per 100,000 population Life expectancy: 78.6 years Infant mortality rate: 5.87 deaths per 1,000 live births All causes of death by age in 2016:

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiology Morbidity And Mortality -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Interventions are also monitored during implementation and evaluated for efficacy, efficiency, impact, cost-effectiveness, and potential for improvement. Two important outcome measures are morbidity and mortality. Changes within these two measures can indicate not only the severity of a health event but also serve as one of the litmus tests for the responses that epidemiologists may take. Morbidity and mortality measures can be gathered using either descriptive or analytic epidemiology and can undergo stratification into various subcategories, such as perinatal, neonatal, infant, and maternal morbidity mortalities, to name a few. Morbidity and mortality can also be stratified by age, race,  ethnicity, sex, gender, nationality, and socioeconomic status, which provide an opportunity to uncover group-specific susceptibilities or exposures within a population.

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiology Morbidity And Mortality -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: On the other hand, mortality is related to the number of deaths caused by the health event under investigation. It can be communicated as a rate or as an absolute number. Mortality usually gets represented as a rate per 1000 individuals, also called the death rate. The calculation for this rate is to divide the number of deaths in a given time for a given population by the total population. To keep these values concise and for ease of comparison to other health events, this number can be multiplied by 1000 to reflect the “per 1000” rate of the target population.

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Infant Death Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), the incidence of SIDS in 2017 was 35.4 per 100,000 live births in the United States. Prior to the introduction of campaigns to reduce SIDS deaths, death rates were noted to vary significantly between Asian populations, aboriginal people in Australia, the population of the United Kingdom, the population of the United States, and the population of New Zealand. Death rates varied from as low as 0.3 deaths per 1000 live births (among Asian populations in the US and Bangladeshi's in the UK) to 7.4 deaths per 100 live births (in the Maori people in New Zealand). Cultural norms, child-rearing practices, and socioeconomic factors appear to explain the variation. [2] In the United States, CDC reports reflect similar variations among non-Hispanic Black Americans, Native Americans, the non-Hispanic white population, Hispanics, and the Asian population. [3] [4] The peak incidence occurs between 2 and 4 months, and 90 percent of cases occur before six months of age. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Meningitis -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In developing countries, the incidence is higher, at 0.8 to 6.1 per 1000 live births, with a mortality rate of up to 58%. The true incidence is likely higher as the epidemiology is limited in many rural, developing settings. [2] [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiology Morbidity And Mortality -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Morbidity and mortality are two types of retrospective information that allows for continuous evaluation of the efficacy of either a specific health care system or an implemented intervention in place. For example, the use of maternal morbidity and mortality to gauge the risks of pregnancy and childbirth, as well as the efficacy of the health care they receive, are of vital importance. [2] In a related vein, accurate assessment of these measures are crucial to understanding and evaluating their impact and trajectory. Ultimately, mortality alongside morbidity allows epidemiologists to study further the burdens that a health event may place on a population. These metrics also allow stakeholders to more effectively prioritize which health events to tackle and allocate resources toward and proactively manage the potential onset of a health event.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Meningitis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Despite the decrease in mortality, neonatal meningitis continues to have high morbidity. Global mortality estimates are approximately 190,000 cases per year. [50] In Western countries, the mortality rate is about 10 to 15%, with the highest rates in preterm neonates. [2] In low-income countries, the mortality rate is as high as 58%, with moderate to severe neurodevelopmental impairment in approximately 23% of survivors. [51] In high-income countries, the rate of neurological sequelae is 20 to 50%. [50] [52] A meta-analysis of neurodevelopmental outcomes in children with GBS meningitis noted that 32% had neurodevelopmental impairment at 18 months, including 18% with moderate to severe impairment. [53] Furthermore, compared to the general population, the risk of death remains higher five years after the acute illness. [50] [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Small for Gestational Age (Archived) -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of FGR SGA varies across different populations. [11] [12] [13] The prevalent rate for FGR SGA is higher in resource-poor countries, with the highest burden in South Asia, where up to 34% of infants were born FGR SGA. [11] [14] In comparison, approximately 11% of infants in rich and developed countries were born FGR SGA. [4] [5] Children born FGR SGA have a higher risk of mortality and morbidity during the neonatal period and beyond. An estimated 22% of neonatal deaths are attributed to being born FGR SGA, with the highest number reported in South Asia. [11] The incidence rates of FGR SGA in preterm infants vary depending on whether data collection was based on birth weight or gestational age. In a cohort of 4438 infants with birthweight from 500 to 1500g, 22% are reported as FGR SGA, according to the National Institute of Child Health and Human Development (NICHD) Neonatal Research Network database. [15] In other cohorts of 20,000 infants with very low birth of < 1500g and gestational age between 25 to 30 weeks, an estimated 9% of these infants were born SGA.

retrieved_docs.title: Fever in a Neonate -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: WHO statistics cite over one million neonatal deaths around the world each year result from neonatal sepsis/pneumonia making it the leading cause of infant mortality. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), the estimated incidence of early-onset neonatal sepsis in the United States is 0.77 to 1 per 1000 live births. With the establishment of guidelines for universal screening and treatment of maternal GBS colonization, the incidence of early-onset sepsis in full-term infants has decreased to 0.3 to 0.4/1000 live births. However, the incidence of late-onset sepsis remained the same (0.4/1000). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Epidemiology Morbidity And Mortality -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: These subcategories provide great insight into the health of the population and highlight any group that may be disproportionately affected. For example, a review of infant mortality in the United States from 1950 to 2010 showed that while infant mortality has substantially decreased in the past four decades, the disparity between African Americans and Whites has progressively increased, further worsened by education and income inequities. [16] Other disparities uncovered include the indirect relationship between socioeconomic status and cancer mortalities, the prevalence of asthma morbidities in inner-city areas, and the unethical targeting of smoking advertisements in low-income schools. [17] [18] [19] It is by using these types of information that stakeholders of a community or population can make better decisions on the type, target, order, and scope of an intervention to pursue, ultimately allowing for communities to properly allocate their time, money, and other resources toward the most impactful and cost-effective interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: Sudden Infant Death Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Siblings of SIDS infants have an increased risk of dying as a result of SIDS. Siblings are 5-6 times more likely to die from SIDS than the general population. [16] [17] [7] After investigation, not all sibling deaths can be attributed to SIDS. Sibling deaths were found to be attributable to inborn errors of metabolism, abuse, and malnourishment.  Twins have a higher incidence of SIDS, about twice as high as singletons. [8] This association is true for preterm twins, term twins, and twins weighing more than 3kg at birth.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Dehydration -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Diarrhea and dehydration cause significant morbidity and mortality in infants and young children worldwide, annually causing approximately 700,000 to 800,000 pediatric deaths, representing nearly 16% of global child fatalities. Childhood diarrhea cases are concentrated in South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa, and the WHO's diarrhea control program successfully reduced worldwide mortality by approximately 75% from the 1980s to 2008. Since then, progress in decreasing mortality has plateaued. [5] In Ethiopia, diarrheal disease kills an estimated 10,000 children aged under age 5 every year. [6] Inadequate sanitation, unsafe water, and lack of access to personal hygiene are responsible for most cases. [7] Pakistan has the highest infant mortality rate in Asia from diarrheal illnesses, with gastroenteritis and dehydration causing approximately 60% of child and infant deaths. [8] In the United States, diarrheal illnesses and dehydration account for more than 200,000 hospitalizations annually in young children but are not a leading cause of mortality. Globally, regions with high breastfeeding rates, safe water, adequate sanitation, and available rotavirus vaccines cause lower rates of pediatric diarrhea and dehydration requiring medical intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Meningitis -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: A study from Tunisia revealed a neurologic complication rate of 21.6%. Respiratory distress, low birth weight, shock, and pleocytosis of fewer than 500 cells/mm3 were indicators of a worse prognosis. Adding ofloxacin to the antibiotic regimen was associated with decreased neurological sequelae in survivors. [55] Another study noted that infant feeding difficulties and concomitant pneumonia were prognostic predictors of poor outcomes. [56] Also, high CSF protein, both during and after acute illness, has been linked to poorer outcomes. [56] [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Birth Asphyxia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of term perinatal asphyxia is 2 per 1000 births in high-resource countries. The rate is 10 times higher in countries with limited maternal and neonatal care access. [7] Of those infants affected, 15% to 20% die in the neonatal period, and up to 25% of survivors are left with permanent neurologic deficits. [8] In preterm infants, the cause of perinatal asphyxia is often unclear, and its incidence can be particularly high, especially in low-resource settings. [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Diarrhea -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: In developed regions, with proper management, the prognosis is very good. However, data reveal an increase in diarrhea-related mortality among US children between the mid-1980s and 2006. Between 2005 and 2007, 1087 diarrhea-related infant deaths were recorded, with 86% of deaths occurring in low birthweight (less than 2500 g) infants. These risk factors included male gender, black ethnicity, and low Apgar score (less than 7). [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Meconium Aspiration -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Mortality in MAS is close to 1.2 percent based on a large retrospective study in the United States. This is lower than the mortality reported in developing countries. [17] The majority of infants recover with a good prognosis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: each condition as a potential adverse event, enabling systematic risk assessment, targeted monitoring, operative decision‑making, and coordinated maternal‑fetal and newborn care aimed at reducing morbidity and improving outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2329
retrieved_docs.content: Infancy is identified as a specific developmental stage that belongs to the broader category of the period of life between birth and death, situating this early phase within the overall lifespan continuum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2850
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology incorporates the concept وفاة حديث الولادة, which denotes neonatal death, as a specific entry within its classification of pediatric adverse events. By including neonatal death in this terminology, healthcare providers can standardize reporting, track incidence, and implement targeted strategies to improve safety and outcomes for newborn patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 752
retrieved_docs.content: essential for clinicians to anticipate potential delivery complications, decide when normal delivery procedures are feasible, and implement appropriate interventions to optimize maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1721
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight, early or threatened labor, threatened preterm labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر), short stature (قصر القامة), and neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة) are interrelated clinical concepts. Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature, indicating that babies born prematurely with low weight are more likely to develop reduced adult height. Short stature itself is clinically associated with neonatal death, suggesting that children who remain short have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Early or threatened labor is used to classify threatened preterm labor, and threatened preterm labor is also clinically associated with short stature. Together, these relationships highlight that low birth weight and threatened preterm labor can contribute to short stature, which in turn is linked to higher neonatal mortality, underscoring the importance of monitoring growth and labor complications in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), indicating that children who remain short may have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Additionally, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that pregnancies complicated by early labor threats often result in offspring who develop short stature. Collectively, these relationships highlight short stature as a common clinical link among preterm low birth weight, threatened preterm labor, and adverse neonatal outcomes such as neonatal death, underscoring its relevance for risk assessment and early intervention in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often develop reduced linear growth. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this vulnerable population may be linked to an increased risk of death shortly after birth. The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses adverse pediatric outcomes, and its subset includes the concepts تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse) and وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). Together, these relationships highlight a network of interrelated risk factors and adverse events: preterm low birth weight, short stature, and neonatal death are closely connected, while umbilical cord prolapse is also recognized within the same adverse‑event framework, underscoring the importance of comprehensive monitoring and intervention in high‑risk newborns.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2523
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature, in turn, is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death). وفاة حديث الولادة is identified as a concept within the Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology, indicating that neonatal death is recognized as a specific adverse event in pediatric care. Together, these relationships suggest that infants born preterm with low birth weight may develop short stature, which is linked to an increased risk of neonatal death, a critical adverse outcome captured by pediatric safety terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.
